Clinical Outcomes: Patients could identify light targets and recognize white objects on a black background, with a restored visual acuity of 20/3240. It was implanted in 6 subjects between 2002-2004.

Electrode Count Or Specs: 60-electrode (6x10 array) epiretinal microelectrode array. The electrodes are 200 µm in diameter, covering a ~20° field of vision.
Clinical Outcomes: Improved basic visual functions such as object localization, motion detection, and letter/word reading in patients with profound vision loss. Best-corrected visual acuity was reported as ~1.8 logMAR or 20/1260. At 5 years, a 40% rate of serious adverse events was reported, including conjunctival erosions and hypotony.
Regulatory Status: Received CE Mark in 2011 and FDA approval in 2013 under a Humanitarian Device Exemption for treating retinitis pigmentosa.
Current Commercial Status: Discontinued. Production was suspended in 2019-2020 due to the company's financial difficulties and a shift in focus to the Orion cortical implant. Support is now managed by Cortigent.

Electrode Count Or Specs: 25-electrode array with protruding electrodes. It is a fully intraocular device. It uses ultrahigh-frequency-pulsed charge-controlled stimulation.
150-microelectrode array on a flexible intraocular foil. The system included a neuromorphic image sensor and an individualized learning retinal encoder.
Clinical Outcomes: Showed improvements in high-contrast object localization, motion detection, and picture recognition in early clinical testing. However, the implants experienced functional decline within 9–12 months, and there were several serious adverse events reported.
Current Commercial Status: Discontinued in 2018 due to a shorter-than-expected device lifespan. The company shifted its focus to the PRIMA subretinal implant.

Developer Company: IntelliMicro Medical (China) and Golden Eye Bionics (US)
Electrode Count Or Specs: 256-electrode array, consisting of 248 electrodes of 210 µm diameter and 8 smaller electrodes of 160 µm diameter. The system includes an episcleral implant body with an antenna for telemetry.
Clinical Outcomes: Clinical trials in five patients reported favorable safety and efficacy, with improvements in visual function tests. The risk of conjunctival breakdown was reported to be low.
Current Commercial Status: Currently undergoing evaluation in human clinical trials.

Electrode Count Or Specs: An array of hundreds of three-dimensional, penetrating microelectrodes (~600). The implant is a wireless, light-powered prosthesis.
Clinical Outcomes: Clinical trial terminated. Preliminary results in late-stage RP patients showed safe implantation and elicitation of finer phosphenes with improved spatial resolution.
Current Commercial Status: Clinical trial was terminated; current status is uncertain.

Electrode Count Or Specs: 10,498 photovoltaic pixels distributed over a wide field of view (~43-46°). It is a foldable and injectable device made from polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS).
Clinical Outcomes: Ex-vivo and in-vivo preclinical studies in mouse and pig models demonstrated the ability to activate retinal ganglion cells at safe irradiance levels with high spatial resolution. The theoretical visual acuity is estimated at 20/480.
Current Commercial Status: Preclinical development. No active clinical trials were noted in the sources.

Electrode Count Or Specs: Alpha IMS: 1500 photodiodes on a 3x3 mm microchip. Alpha AMS: 1600 photodiodes on a 3.2x4 mm chip. Both are active subretinal implants powered by an external source.
Clinical Outcomes: Improved light perception, object identification, and activities of daily living. The highest reported visual acuity was 20/546 with the Alpha AMS. However, the implants had a limited lifespan, with the Alpha IMS failing due to encapsulation issues.
Regulatory Status: CE marked in Europe (Alpha IMS in 2013, Alpha AMS in 2016). Not FDA-approved.
Current Commercial Status: Discontinued. The company, Retina Implant AG, was dissolved in March 2019 due to economic and performance issues. The devices were never commercialized in the US. Research on the Alpha AMS continues with academic partners.

Electrode Count Or Specs: A 2 mm wide, 25 µm thick passive microphotodiode array with approximately 5000 microphotodiodes.
Clinical Outcomes: The device showed a neuroprotective effect, leading to unexpected improvements in visual acuity, color/contrast perception, and visual field size in some patients. However, it produced inadequate stimulating current from ambient light alone.
Current Commercial Status: Discontinued. The company ceased operations, and the project was discontinued.

Electrode Count Or Specs: A wireless photovoltaic subretinal microchip, 2x2 mm in size and 30 µm thick, containing 378 pixels (electrodes). It is activated by near-infrared light projected from external glasses.
Clinical Outcomes: Clinical trials in patients with geographic atrophy (dry AMD) have shown clinically meaningful improvements in visual acuity, with some patients able to read letters. The average acuity ranges from 20/438 to 20/550, closely matching the 100 µm pixel size. The device has been shown to be well-tolerated with stable function for up to 4 years.
Regulatory Status: FDA Breakthrough Device Designation. Clinical trials are underway in Europe and the U.S. A CE mark application is planned.
Current Commercial Status: In pivotal clinical trials (PRIMAvera study) in Europe and the U.S. to support regulatory submissions. The assets of the original developer, Pixium Vision, were acquired by Science Corporation in 2024.

Developer Company: Bionic Vision Technologies (BVT) / Bionic Vision Australia (BVA)
Electrode Count Or Specs: Prototypes have included 24-channel, 44-channel, and 98/99-channel (Phoenix 99) arrays of platinum disc electrodes. Gen 2 is a fully implantable 44-channel device.
Clinical Outcomes: Early clinical trials have shown that the implants elicit phosphenes and can improve performance in localization and mobility tasks. A Phase II trial of a 44-channel device reported no serious adverse events and significant improvements in functional assessments. One study reported a measurable grating acuity of 20/8397.
Regulatory Status: In Phase I/II clinical trials in Australia. Has received FDA Breakthrough Device Designation.
Current Commercial Status: In clinical development. Multiple generations of devices (e.g., 24-channel, 44-channel, 99-channel Phoenix 99) have been trialed or are in preclinical/clinical stages. Not commercially available.

Electrode Count Or Specs: A 3D array of 49 microelectrodes that protrude to penetrate the sclera, placing the electrodes closer to the retina. An earlier prototype used a 9-electrode implant.
Clinical Outcomes: Pilot studies showed that patients could identify and discriminate objects and detect motion better than by chance. The safety profile was reported as reassuring, with no serious adverse events requiring further surgery at 1-year follow-up.
Current Commercial Status: Clinical development. At least two generations of the device have been tested in clinical trials. Further investigation is still required.

Electrode Count Or Specs: Not an electrode-based device. It is a dual-component therapy combining a gene therapy product (GS030-DP) delivered via AAV2 vector to express the light-sensitive opsin ChrimsonR in ganglion cells, and light-stimulating goggles (GS030-MD).
Clinical Outcomes: Phase 1/2 PIONEER trial showed that at 1 year, some patients improved from light perception to being able to locate and count objects, particularly at the highest dose. Case reports have detailed partial recovery of visual function, allowing patients to perceive and locate objects with the aid of the stimulating goggles. The treatment has been generally well-tolerated, with manageable intraocular inflammation reported.
Regulatory Status: Has received FDA Fast Track Designation and Orphan Drug Designation in both the U.S. and Europe. Currently in Phase I/II clinical trials.
Current Commercial Status: In clinical development (Phase I/II). The company is accelerating the program to become its second product to reach the market.

Electrode Count Or Specs: Not an electrode-based device. It is an ambient-light activatable multi-characteristic opsin (MCO) gene therapy delivered via a single intravitreal injection of an AAV2 vector. It does not require external stimulation devices.
Clinical Outcomes: The Phase 2b RESTORE clinical trial showed a statistically significant improvement in best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) at week 52 compared to a sham control. Long-term data shows sustained or enhanced vision-related quality-of-life scores through 5 years. The therapy was well-tolerated with no serious adverse events reported.
Regulatory Status: Has received FDA Fast Track, Orphan Drug, and RMAT designations, as well as EMA Orphan designations. A rolling BLA has been initiated with the FDA.
Current Commercial Status: Nanoscope has initiated a rolling Biologics License Application (BLA) submission to the FDA, indicating it is in the late stages of clinical development and moving toward commercialization.

Developer Company: Bionic Sight, in collaboration with Applied Genetic Therapies Corp. (AGTC). AGTC was later acquired by Syncona.
Electrode Count Or Specs: Not an electrode-based device. It is an optogenetic gene therapy vector that delivers proteins to the optic nerve. It is used in conjunction with a device that utilizes the retina's neural code.
Clinical Outcomes: In a Phase 1/2 study, the first four blind patients treated were reported to be able to detect light and motion. Two of these patients were also able to detect the direction of motion. No further quantitative results are available in the sources.
Regulatory Status: Currently undergoing Phase I/II clinical trial for retinitis pigmentosa.
Current Commercial Status: In clinical development (Phase I/II). Not commercially available.

Device Name: Hybrid Retinal Implant (hESC-PRP seeded electrode array)
Electrode Count Or Specs: High-density array with a pixel size down to 10 µm. Features 3D micro-wells to create a tight "tight-seal" neuron-electrode coupling, which is shown to reduce the activation threshold by three orders of magnitude to the picocoulomb (pC) level. A 1 mm diameter implant for in vivo rat studies contained 3,172 micro-well electrode complexes.
Clinical Outcomes: Preclinical in vitro and in vivo studies in rats demonstrated survival of transplanted photoreceptor precursors for over a month, with axonal elongation toward the inner retina and formation of putative synapses. In vitro tests showed that the coupled neurons could be activated by the electric field.
Current Commercial Status: Preclinical research stage; not a commercial product. It is presented as a novel approach to overcome the limitations of current prostheses.

Developer Company: Tohru Yagi's research group and collaborators (Japan). Supported by Terumo Life Science Foundation and Asahi Glass Foundation.
Electrode Count Or Specs: MEMS-based micro/nanoelectrode arrays, including conductive polymer versions. An early multielectrode array had 9 pairs of 20x20 µm stimulating sites with 40 µm spacing on a 20x6 mm substrate. Axons from attached neurons are guided by a peripheral nerve graft.
Clinical Outcomes: Preclinical in vitro and in vivo (rodent) experiments reported, including nerve cell culture, axon guidance, and electrical stimulation. The goal is to use the attached neurons as "living electric wires." No clinical outcomes reported.
Current Commercial Status: Early-stage research prototype. A first prototype with external and internal MEMS devices has been developed. Expected to reach a clinical stage in 20-30 years.

Developer Company: Various academic groups (e.g., RIKEN in Japan, Seiler Lab at UC Irvine). AIVITA Biomedical is noted as a collaborator in some studies.
Electrode Count Or Specs: Not applicable (biological tissue graft). The approach uses sheets of retinal tissue dissected from 3D retinal organoids derived from human pluripotent stem cells. Some studies use supportive scaffolds like parylene.
Clinical Outcomes: Transplantation of hESC-derived retinal sheets into primate and rodent models has shown graft survival, maturation into photoreceptors, integration, and some restoration of visual function in animals. Early human clinical applications have demonstrated safety and stable graft survival over 2 years, with potential functional improvement in one patient.
Regulatory Status: Early-phase clinical trials are underway. A phase 1/2a study for a composite RPE implant (CPCB-RPE1) is FDA-cleared. A clinical study for organoid sheets began in Japan in 2020.
Current Commercial Status: Preclinical and early clinical research stage. Not a commercial product. Commercialization faces significant obstacles.

Developer Company: D. Kacy Cullen's research group (University of Pennsylvania) and collaborators.
Electrode Count Or Specs: Engineered constructs of living cortical neurons and axonal tracts encased in soft hydrogel cylinders. The constructs are on the order of several hundred microns in diameter and can be made up to several centimeters long. Designed to be coupled with external micro-electrocorticography (\u00b5ECoG) or optical arrays.
Clinical Outcomes: Pre-clinical demonstration of signal propagation and neuronal survival. *In vivo* implantation in rats showed survival up to 1 month with evidence of synaptogenesis. *In vitro* studies showed simultaneous optical stimulation and recording capabilities.
Current Commercial Status: Research prototype/preclinical stage. University of Pennsylvania spin-out companies (INNERVACE, Axonova Medical) are noted for translation of regenerative therapies. Not a commercial device.
